dna-reader.readerDescription: Mid-career professional, 30–45, technically competent but overwhelmed by competing priorities. Has read productivity books before (GTD, Essentialism) but found them too system-heavy to sustain. Reads on commutes and before bed. Wants permission to simplify, not another system to maintain.
dna-reader.readerProblem: Overwhelmed by volume of work and unable to focus on what actually matters long-term
dna-reader.existingKnowledge: Familiar with GTD, time-blocking, the Eisenhower matrix. Has tried and abandoned several systems.
objections: Another productivity book won't help — I've tried everything
My job is too chaotic for any system to work
I don't have time to implement a new approach

dna-transform.beforeState: Reacts to everything, owns nothing. Ends each day with a list of done tasks and a feeling of having missed what mattered.
dna-transform.afterState: Has a reliable daily practice for identifying one thing that actually matters, and a framework for saying no to everything else without guilt.

dna-idea.bigIdea: Clarity is not a talent or a personality trait — it is a daily practice of subtraction, and anyone can learn it.
dna-idea.oneLineSummary: The clear person is not smarter, they just remove more noise.

dna-author.authorAngle: 10 years as a strategy consultant helping executives cut through organisational noise to find the one decision that matters per quarter. The insight: the tools that work for CEOs work for individuals — they're just never taught at the personal level.

dna-promise.corePromise: A 15-minute daily practice that cuts decision noise and frees mental capacity for what actually matters.
dna-promise.workingSubtitle: The One-Sentence Daily Practice That Cuts Noise and Restores Focus

comps.title: Essentialism
comps.author: Greg McKeown
comps.whatItDoes: Permission to do less; strong philosophy
comps.gap: No practical daily mechanism — philosophy without implementation

comps.title: Deep Work
comps.author: Cal Newport
comps.gap: Assumes reader has scheduling control; doesn't address reactive work environments

comps.title: Four Thousand Weeks
comps.author: Oliver Burkeman
comps.whatItDoes: Reframes time as finite and meaning-laden
comps.gap: Philosophical — reader feels better but doesn't know what to do differently on Monday

dna-comps.gap: All three shift mindset but none give a repeatable daily practice. The Clarity Habit is the operational layer that Essentialism, Deep Work, and Four Thousand Weeks need.

dna-voice.voiceRange: Warmest: the worked examples showing what clarity looks like in practice. Most rigorous: the critique of conventional productivity advice.

dna-evidence.uncertaintyHandling: Distinguish clearly between observed patterns from consulting work and peer-reviewed claims. Use 'in my experience' for the former, cite sources for the latter.

dna-commercial.targetAudience: Executives and senior professionals who could become consulting clients
dna-commercial.successMetric: 5,000 sales in 12 months; 10 new consulting inquiries attributable to the book

workingTitles: Subtract: The Daily Practice of Clarity

dna-consolidate.positioningStatement: For overwhelmed mid-career professionals who have tried productivity systems and found them unsustainable, The Clarity Habit teaches a 15-minute daily subtraction practice — derived from executive strategy work — that reliably surfaces the one thing that matters. Unlike Essentialism or Deep Work, it provides an operational daily mechanism, not just a philosophy.

pa-thesis.thesis: Clarity is a practice of subtraction, not addition — and anyone can learn it in 15 minutes a day.

objections.objection: My job is too reactive for any daily practice
I've tried clarity practices and they don't stick
15 minutes isn't enough time to matter

principles.name: The Subtraction Principle
principles.claim: Every decision you remove creates capacity for a better one

principles.name: The Reveal Principle
principles.claim: What matters most is invisible until noise is removed

principles.name: The Commitment Principle
principles.claim: Clarity without a committed response is just planning
